raw:
[HIGH] Patch values are typed and validated as `unknown`, so non-JSON values are accepted
src/types.ts:4: `value: z.unknown()` permits `undefined`, functions, symbols, `NaN`, and other values that cannot exist in a JSON Patch document. The inferred `PatchOp` type also exposes `value: unknown`, so TypeScript does not enforce the library's own `JsonValue` model.
Define a recursive `JsonValue` Zod schema and use it for `add`, `replace`, and `test` values.

[HIGH] Pointer fields accept invalid RFC 6901 paths
src/types.ts:4: `path: z.string()` accepts strings like `"foo"` or `"a/b"`, but a JSON Pointer must be either `""` or start with `/`; the same issue applies to `from` on `move` and `copy`. If downstream code assumes schema validation handled op shape, invalid patch documents get through.
Validate pointer strings with a refinement such as `p === "" || p.startsWith("/")` for both `path` and `from`.

src/types.ts:4: `value: z.unknown()` permits `undefined`, functions, symbols, `NaN`, and other values that cannot exist in a JSON Patch document. The inferred `PatchOp` type also exposes `value: unknown`, so TypeScript does not enforce the library's own `JsonValue` model.
Define a recursive `JsonValue` Zod schema and use it for `add`, `replace`, and `test` values.

Pointer fields accept invalid RFC 6901 paths
src/types.ts:4: `path: z.string()` accepts strings like `"foo"` or `"a/b"`, but a JSON Pointer must be either `""` or start with `/`; the same issue applies to `from` on `move` and `copy`. If downstream code assumes schema validation handled op shape, invalid patch documents get through.
Validate pointer strings with a refinement such as `p === "" || p.startsWith("/")` for both `path` and `from`.

raw:
[HIGH] JSON Pointer escape sequences are not decoded
/Users/anton_personal/GitHub/ask-llm-codex-pair-experiment/runs-task-3/run-B-v2/src/patch.ts:8: `parsePath` splits on `/` but never converts `~1` to `/` or `~0` to `~`, so RFC 6901 paths like `/a~1b` resolve to the wrong property.
Decode each token after splitting, and reject invalid `~` escape sequences.

[HIGH] `applyPatch` mutates the caller's input and is not atomic on failure
/Users/anton_personal/GitHub/ask-llm-codex-pair-experiment/runs-task-3/run-B-v2/src/patch.ts:102: `result` starts as `doc`, and helpers mutate arrays/objects in place; if a later operation throws, earlier mutations remain visible on the original document.
Deep-clone the input before applying operations, and only return the cloned result after all operations succeed.

[HIGH] `test` uses reference equality instead of JSON structural equality
/Users/anton_personal/GitHub/ask-llm-codex-pair-experiment/runs-task-3/run-B-v2/src/patch.ts:92: `actual !== op.value` fails valid JSON Patch tests for structurally equal objects and arrays, e.g. `{a:1}` vs `{a:1}`.
Use a JSON deep-equality comparison for `test`.

[HIGH] `remove` succeeds on missing object properties
/Users/anton_personal/GitHub/ask-llm-codex-pair-experiment/runs-task-3/run-B-v2/src/patch.ts:58: `delete obj[head]` does not check that the member exists, but RFC 6902 requires `remove` to error when the target location does not exist.
Check own-property existence before deletion and throw if missing.

[HIGH] `replace` incorrectly creates missing paths
/Users/anton_personal/GitHub/ask-llm-codex-pair-experiment/runs-task-3/run-B-v2/src/patch.ts:75: `replace` is routed through `setValueAt`, which creates object members and sparse array slots instead of requiring the target location to already exist.
Validate that the full target path exists before setting for `replace`.

[HIGH] Array `add` does assignment instead of insertion and mishandles `-`
/Users/anton_personal/GitHub/ask-llm-codex-pair-experiment/runs-task-3/run-B-v2/src/patch.ts:31: For arrays, `add` must insert before the specified index and `-` must append, but `doc[idx] = value` overwrites existing elements and `parseInt("-", 10)` produces `NaN`.
Implement array-specific `add` using `splice(index, 0, value)` and append handling for `-`.

[HIGH] Invalid array indexes are accepted or produce wrong mutations
/Users/anton_personal/GitHub/ask-llm-codex-pair-experiment/runs-task-3/run-B-v2/src/patch.ts:15: Array paths are resolved with unchecked `parseInt`, so tokens like `1abc`, `-`, out-of-range indexes, negative indexes, and leading-zero forms are not rejected correctly.
Parse array indexes with strict RFC-compatible validation and bounds checks per operation.

[HIGH] `move` allows moving a value into its own descendant
/Users/anton_personal/GitHub/ask-llm-codex-pair-experiment/runs-task-3/run-B-v2/src/patch.ts:79: There is no check that `from` is not a prefix of `path`, so invalid moves like moving `/a` to `/a/b` are attempted and can corrupt or lose data.
Before executing `move`, reject operations where the destination path is inside the source path.

[HIGH] `copy` aliases objects instead of copying JSON values
/Users/anton_personal/GitHub/ask-llm-codex-pair-experiment/runs-task-3/run-B-v2/src/patch.ts:87: `copy` retrieves the source value and writes the same reference, so later mutations through either location can affect both branches in the returned document.
Deep-clone the copied value before inserting it.

[HIGH] Prototype pollution is possible through object paths
/Users/anton_personal/GitHub/ask-llm-codex-pair-experiment/runs-task-3/run-B-v2/src/patch.ts:40: Assigning arbitrary path tokens onto plain objects permits paths like `/__proto__/polluted`, which can mutate object prototypes.
Reject dangerous keys such as `__proto__`, `prototype`, and `constructor` when traversing or writing object paths.

raw:
[HIGH] Missing JSON Pointer escape-sequence coverage
tests/patch.test.ts:5: The suite never tests `~0` or `~1`, so an implementation that incorrectly treats `/a~1b` as a literal key would pass. This misses a required RFC 6901 path-resolution behavior.
Add tests for escaped keys like `/a~1b` resolving to `a/b` and `/m~0n` resolving to `m~n`.

[HIGH] `test` op only covers primitive equality
tests/patch.test.ts:21: The suite only verifies primitive equality, so an implementation using `===` would pass while failing required structural equality for objects and arrays. RFC 6902 `test` requires deep equality.
Add passing and failing `test` cases for nested objects and arrays.

[HIGH] No atomicity or input-mutation coverage
tests/patch.test.ts:41: The multi-op test only checks success and does not verify that a later failure leaves the caller's original document unchanged. A mutating implementation that partially applies operations before throwing would pass this suite.
Add a multi-op failure test that asserts the original input object is unchanged after `applyPatch` throws.

[HIGH] Missing array `-` semantics coverage
tests/patch.test.ts:5: There are no array tests for `path: "/-"`, so invalid handling of append versus existing-element operations would pass. RFC 6902 allows `-` for `add` only and rejects it for `remove`, `replace`, `move from`, `copy from`, and `test`.
Add tests that `add` appends with `/-` and all other uses of `-` throw.

[HIGH] Missing invalid path behavior coverage
tests/patch.test.ts:11: The suite only tests successful `remove` and `replace` paths and never verifies failures on missing targets. RFC 6902 requires `remove`, `replace`, `copy from`, `move from`, and `test` to error when the referenced location does not exist.
Add negative tests for missing object keys and out-of-range array indexes across those operations.

[HIGH] Missing `move` self-prefix rejection coverage
tests/patch.test.ts:31: The only `move` test moves a scalar between sibling object keys and does not cover moving a value into its own descendant. RFC 6902 requires `from` being a prefix of `path` to fail.
Add a test such as moving from `/a` to `/a/b` and assert it throws.

[HIGH] Missing prototype-pollution coverage
tests/patch.test.ts:5: The suite never exercises dangerous path tokens like `__proto__`, `constructor`, or `prototype`, so a vulnerable implementation could pass while polluting global object prototypes. This is explicitly part of the stated threat surface.
Add tests asserting crafted paths like `/__proto__/polluted` throw and do not modify `{}` or `Object.prototype`.
